Okay, part six, firing 101. First, I want to talk about when to fire somebody, okay? So when do you know that you need to let someone go? Disclaimer before we get into this. Effective managers know how to hire someone and they also know how to fire somebody, okay? So the only thing worse than firing someone is keeping them on the team because of your emotions and because the fact that you don't feel like being uncomfortable and letting them go and embracing the change that brings to your team, okay? And so what you must know is that your job as a manager, as a leader is to choose and know when to fire someone, okay? This is something that you must master, you must get good at, okay? And you must not be afraid to make decisions. The worst thing that you can do as a business owner is be scared of making the wrong decision because inevitably you will default to make the wrong decision because you sit on it for so long, okay? So when is an appropriate time to fire someone? One is they're not meeting performance standards. Say you've gone through three write-ups now or you've gone through two and you know it's just done and they're not meeting the performance standards or you have a sales guy and he consistently is closing 15% of all people walking through the door, right? That you can't sustain a business that way. Two, they tear down the team and they're not a cultural fit. They don't represent your values, other people don't like them, other people avoid them purposely because they're not nice or they are a bad attitude, something like that, right? Three, rules are not suggestions. So you have rules, you have guidelines and say that person has ample time to correct a problem or issue but they don't and they need to pursue a career elsewhere because if they won't listen to you and they won't respect the fact that there are rules, processes and systems in place to protect the business, then they cannot be a part of your team. Four, they're apathetic, meaning they don't care about the customers, they don't care about the company, they don't care about anything else. So why should you care about that, right? Somebody who's apathetic, meaning that they're just doing it probably for a paycheck, they probably don't actually care about your customers, they're not involved in the vision, right? And lastly, somebody who thrives on drama, they take nourishment from hurting others' feelings, they disc the company at every opportunity to clients behind your back, they spread gossip, like absolutely unacceptable. And if you're thinking to yourself, oh, I think everyone spreads gossip, well, then it's probably you who spreads gossip and you should probably change that because nobody in our company spreads gossip. So now we've talked about when to fire someone, let's look at how to fire that person. So you understand who you need to fire, now it's like, okay, how do I do that so that I protect myself, right? And I'm very clear. How? One, do not let emotional rule here, okay? You need to create a plan, you need to pick a day and a time that you're gonna fire this person. So you need to be very logical, you can't just go off the whim and be like, I'm firing them right now, I'm so pissed off. Like don't do that, okay? You need to protect yourself. So if you feel that way, go cool down for five minutes, come back and be like, all right, today's not the day, but we need to do it in the next two days. And we need to create action plan around it, right? I suggest doing it early in the day and early in the week because it encourages people to go look for another job. The weekends, a lot of times they'll spend moping around and then maybe even plotting revenge or slamming you on social media. So I suggest months and you're not going to feel that great about it. And so I want you to make sure that you're written down first and you're rehearsed. I'm not kidding. I rehearsed all of my firings until I got really good at it and then it just became like clockwork. That sounds terrible. But until it became to the point where I was comfortable enough to not need a script. So you need to recognize what you're up to. It's not a counseling session, this is an announcement. It's an irrevocable decision. The change has been made, like they're fired, there's no discussion to be had here. And so the first thing is you want to get right to the point. You're gonna skip the small talk, you're gonna start the termination meeting by saying, hello, John, can you sit down? Like I've got to talk to you about something. And then announcing right from the start, like this will cause them to pay attention because your tone is different. You're like, hey, we've got to talk. Second is you're gonna break the bad news. Like it's not like, hey, John, we've got to talk. Like, how are you doing today though? It's not that it's, hey, John, we've got to talk. I gotta tell you something, your employment's been terminated. And it's not will be terminated or is going to be terminated. You are and have been terminated. Four, next step is you do not engage in conversation. If they continue to ask questions, inform them that their employment has ended and ask if they have questions regarding the next steps of their termination, rather than what has happened. We're not discussing what has happened. Do not discuss the termination further. Just what needs to be done to tie up loose ends. This is to protect you legally. Five, cover everything essential. Okay, so be specific about what will happen next. Their pay, their benefits, unused vacation, references, will you be a reference for them, out placement, explanations to coworkers, projects, et cetera. This is one where you can't say, I'll get back to you on that. You need to go through firing checklist to see where you will most likely need to be removing them or you need to cross your T's, dot your I's, and then you make sure that they're 100% removed within 60 minutes of firing. So a lot of the times, if you do this very early in the morning on a Monday, you can get a lot of the access removed prior to letting them go and then once it's done, then you can do anything further that they will be able to visibly see or would have been able to notice prior to the conversation. So now that you have fired this person, you know that you fired the right person, you've gone through with it, how are you going to tell your team? So one, you shouldn't tell exact specifics. Like as if some of, what I want you to understand is that some of what is happening is confidential and you need to be mindful of that. So you cannot tell exact situations, you can give examples and roundabout answers. So how do you do this? One, you wanna tell them as quickly as possible and you would try to avoid email. So I suggest doing this on a daily, huddle, weekly meeting, whatever the next, the closest here is getting fired, Johnny was fired, but I have to let you all know why and I just want you to feel okay to ask questions because now's the time. And then you basically open it up for questions, you let anyone and everyone ask about what happened and you explain to them, hey team, I just wanna let you know like now's the time to ask the questions about Johnny. However, after we end this call in seven minutes, we are no longer going to discuss his termination and we are no longer going to talk about Johnny, good or bad, okay? And that is how you tell the team.
